channel.title: VLA models on HC's Log
channel.description: Recent content in VLA models on HC's Log

item.title: RT-2
item.description:
RT-2: Vision-Language-Action Models Transfer Web Knowledge to Robotic Control

Authors: Anthony Brohan et al.

Organization: Google DeepMind Year: 2023

Paper / Blog https://deepmind.google/discover/blog/rt-2-new-model-translates-vision-and-language-into-action/

Problem

Robot policies trained purely on robot datasets often struggle to generalize to new tasks.

Most robot learning systems rely on:

  • imitation learning
  • reinforcement learning
  • robot demonstration datasets

However, robot datasets are typically orders of magnitude smaller than the web-scale datasets used to train modern vision and language models.

item.title: π0
item.description:
π0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control

Organization: Physical Intelligence Year: 2024 Paper: https://www.physicalintelligence.company/download/pi0.pdf

Problem

Most recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models treat robot actions as discrete tokens, similar to text tokens in language models.

Examples include models such as:

  • RT-2
  • OpenVLA
  • Octo

While tokenization simplifies modeling, it introduces several limitations for robot control:

  • discretization can reduce control precision
  • action tokens may produce jerky or unnatural trajectories
  • continuous robot motion must be approximated using symbolic outputs

In practice, robot control is inherently continuous and dynamic. Policies must generate smooth trajectories that respect physical constraints.

item.title: π0.5
item.description:
π0.5: Hierarchical Vision-Language-Action Flow Model

Organization: Physical Intelligence Year: 2025

Problem

Early Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models such as RT-1, RT-2 and π0 demonstrate that robot policies can be learned from large datasets using transformer-based architectures.

However, these systems still struggle with long-horizon manipulation tasks.

Typical problems include:

  • tasks requiring many sequential steps
  • complex household environments
  • generalization to new homes or scenes
  • difficulty maintaining coherent plans over long time horizons

Low-level policies alone often fail because they attempt to directly map observations to actions without an explicit notion of task structure.
